nativeRange.habitat: Short, fast-flowing coastal streams on tropical, often volcanic islands; characteristically found in runs and riffles above waterfalls and cataracts, where larvae cannot drift back down. Substrate is bedrock and rounded stones with rich biofilm and diatom cover.
nativeRange.biotope: Tropical island coastal stream above waterfalls

decorPreferences: smooth water-worn rocks, rounded pebbles, and small boulders for grazing surfaces
fine sand or smooth fine gravel substrate
hardy low-light epiphytes (Anubias, Microsorum, Bolbitis) anchored to rocks; mature algae growth strongly encouraged
powerhead or strong canister return aimed lengthwise to produce visible riffle-like surface current and high dissolved oxygen
tightly-fitting lid — these fish climb glass and emersed surfaces using their fused pelvic suction disc

diet.notes: The Stiphodon Goby is a specialized bottom-feeding algae grazer, using its subterminal, extrusible mouth — adapted for scraping rather than biting — to feed on the thin film of biofilm, diatoms, and soft green algae that coats submerged rocks and wood. Keeping these fish well fed depends on having a biologically mature aquarium; tanks that have been running for several months will develop the natural algae growth they rely on, while newly set-up tanks routinely fail to sustain them. Sinking algae wafers, Repashy-style gel pastes (such as Soilent Green or Morning Wood), and small frozen items such as Cyclops, baby brine shrimp, or finely chopped bloodworm are accepted as supplements, but prepared foods alone are not enough to meet their needs. Tough filamentous algae and cyanobacteria are generally not preferred. If natural algae growth is sparse, blanched vegetables such as spinach or courgette can also be offered alongside algae wafers.

goodWith: peaceful stream-dwelling nano fish (White Cloud Mountain Minnows, small Danio, Microdevario, Tanichthys)
hillstream loaches (Gastromyzon, Pseudogastromyzon, Sewellia) sharing the high-flow preference
small Rasbora and pencilfish that occupy upper water column
freshwater shrimp (Neocaridina, Caridina) — gobies ignore adult shrimp
small peaceful Corydoras in the cooler end of their range
other Stiphodon species — they coexist amicably in the wild and in aquaria
avoidWith: aggressive bottom-feeders (Schistura, larger Botia loaches) that will out-compete them for food and territory
territorial Rhinogobius gobies that monopolise bottom territory
cichlids and other territorial or predatory species
fin-nippers (tiger barbs, serpae tetras) — Stiphodon are slow grazers and easily harassed
fast-moving aggressive feeders that strip the rockwork before the gobies feed

imageCandidates.notes: Laurent Pasteur, 3608x2405. Male S. percnopterygionus (accepted synonym in alsoKnownAs) showing textbook electric blue neon lateral stripe, blue head markings and reddish dorsal-fin margin on a live vibrant fish grazing algae rock — the exact Cobalt/Neon Blue Goby look that names this entry; tack sharp, whole animal framed.

Laurent Pasteur, 3958x2633. Male S. ornatus (accepted synonym) in full nuptial display with all fins flared — orange-patterned dorsal/pectoral fins, green cheek, spotted body. Extremely striking, crisp and well-lit; adds genus variety.

Laurent Pasteur, 3317x2211. Male S. percnopterygionus (accepted synonym) showing the gold/yellow neon lateral stripe morph with red dorsal accents; sharp, live, vibrant, representing the Gold Neon Dwarf Goby variant.

summary: The Stiphodon Goby is a small, peaceful algae-grazing stream fish native to fast coastal streams across East and Southeast Asia. Several species — including S. atropurpureus, S. semoni, and S. ornatus — are sold interchangeably as Cobalt Blue or Neon Blue Goby. All require a mature, algae-rich, high-flow tank and a tightly fitting lid, as they are accomplished climbers.
careNotes: This is an umbrella entry covering the small algae-grazing Stiphodon species commonly imported under hobby names including Cobalt Blue Goby, Neon Blue Goby, Blue Neon Dwarf Goby, and Gold Neon Dwarf Goby. The trade rarely identifies them precisely, and shipments labelled 'Stiphodon sp.' typically include S. atropurpureus, S. semoni, S. ornatus, S. percnopterygionus, and occasionally S. elegans. All members of the genus share virtually identical husbandry, so they can be discussed and kept together. Stiphodon are amphidromous stream-dwellers from short, fast coastal streams on volcanic and tropical islands across Japan, Taiwan, southern China, the Philippines, Indonesia, New Guinea, and the Solomons. In the wild they live above waterfalls in clear, rapid, oxygen-saturated runs grazing diatom and biofilm films from bedrock. Successfully replicating those conditions is the central husbandry task: provide a tank of at least 75 litres with a 60 cm or longer footprint, strong directional flow (a powerhead or canister return aimed lengthwise targeting 10-15x tank turnover per hour is widely recommended, though 5-10x is acceptable with vigorous surface aeration), and a substrate of fine sand or smooth gravel with abundant smooth rounded stones and pebbles arranged to create varied grazing surfaces. Crucially, the tank must be biologically mature before adding Stiphodon: they are obligate biofilm/diatom grazers and routinely starve in newly cycled aquariums. A well-lit tank with visible diatom and soft green algae cover on stones is ideal. Supplement with algae wafers, Repashy-style gel foods, and occasional frozen Cyclops or bloodworm, but do not rely on prepared foods. They are generally considered peaceful — males are loosely territorial, especially during breeding, but disputes are mild and consist mostly of display rather than damage. A single male with two or more females is generally recommended; small mixed-species groups of 3-6 individuals also work well and replicate the loose aggregations seen in the wild. Stiphodon are accomplished climbers — their fused pelvic fins form a suction disc that lets them ascend wet glass and even waterfalls. A tightly fitting lid is essential; gaps around filter inlets, heaters, and cable cut-outs must be sealed or covered with mesh. Sexual dimorphism is striking in mature fish: males show vivid blue, green, or gold lateral neon stripes, reddish dorsal-fin margins, and a white fatty patch behind the pectoral fins, while females are cryptic browns and tans with a zig-zag lateral marking. Water quality must remain high; these are sensitive to nitrate accumulation and low oxygen, so weekly partial water changes of around 30 percent and high dissolved-oxygen saturation are advised. Conservation note: several Stiphodon species are of subtropical Asian conservation concern due to dam-blocked migration and aquarium-trade collection; sourcing from sustainable suppliers is preferable.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been achieved for any Stiphodon species. The genus is amphidromous: adults spawn in freshwater and males guard the small adhesive eggs (which can number up to 10,000 per spawning, ~0.5 mm in diameter) for less than 24 hours until hatching, after which the pelagic larvae must drift downstream to the sea, where they spend 78-146 days as marine plankton before juveniles migrate back upstream and over waterfalls into freshwater. The marine larval phase cannot currently be replicated in home aquaria, so all hobby specimens are wild-caught. Spawning behaviour is regularly observed in well-conditioned captive groups (June-November in nature, but year-round in stable aquaria), but the larvae invariably die within days. Sexual maturity is reached within roughly one year.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Stiphodon atropurpureus
primary.notes: Primary source for water parameters, tank requirements, native range, biotope, diet, breeding lifecycle, sexual dimorphism, climbing/escape risk, and trade-name synonymy. Confirms 4-5 cm adult size, 22-26°C, pH 6.5-7.5, gH 36-215 ppm, 60x30 cm minimum footprint, peaceful temperament with loose male territoriality, 1M:2F grouping, and the absence of any successful captive rearing of fry.

additional.name: SeriouslyFish — Stiphodon semoni
additional.notes: Sister-species cross-check for the umbrella entry. Confirms essentially identical husbandry: 4-5 cm adult size, 22-28°C, pH 6.5-7.5, gH 36-215 ppm, 60x30 cm/54 L minimum, peaceful with loose male territoriality, 1M:2F minimum, amphidromous lifecycle with no captive larval survival, glass-climbing requiring tight cover.

additional.name: FishBase — Stiphodon atropurpureus
additional.notes: Taxonomic and ecological cross-check. Confirms max length 5.0 cm TL, family Gobiidae, order Gobiiformes (note: SeriouslyFish still lists Perciformes — Gobiiformes is the currently accepted order). Confirms Northwest Pacific distribution (Japan, Taiwan, China, Philippines, Malaysia), amphidromous lifecycle, and biofilm/epiphyte diet on stream stones. Flags subtropical-Asia conservation concern from dam-induced migration blockage and aquarium-trade collection.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Selecting the Right Algae Eater
additional.notes: Designated primary source for FW fish slice; only mentions Stiphodon in passing as a school-friendly algae eater for medium tanks alongside Otocinclus and panda garras. No species-specific care details. Aquarium Co-Op has no dedicated Stiphodon care guide or product page, so SeriouslyFish was elevated to primary.

additional.name: Aquatic Arts — Blue/Gold Neon Dwarf Goby product pages
additional.notes: Retail husbandry cross-check via web search summary: confirms 20 US gal (~75 L) minimum for a small group, 72-78°F (22-26°C), pH 6.5-7.5, very high turnover (10-15x), powerhead-driven flow, fine sand or smooth gravel substrate, algae/biofilm primary diet supplemented with sinking pellets/wafers, and sensitivity to declining water quality. Aligns closely with SeriouslyFish numbers.

fish.breedingNotes: Amphidromous: eggs hatch into pelagic marine larvae that cannot be raised in freshwater aquaria. Spawning regularly observed in mature captive groups but no Stiphodon species has been successfully reared to juvenile from captive-spawned eggs.
